notes: Built from the VIGIL causal skeleton library.
All entities are synthetic while preserving the causal logic of each source skeleton.
Designed to satisfy the Track 1 checklist: hidden structure, disconfirmation, behavior-first scoring, and contamination resistance.

scenarios.title: Rillfen Marsh Bloom-Turn Collapse
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A marsh authority asks you to determine why several small reed-fin species collapsed after a seasonal algal event in Rillfen Marsh. The obvious answer is already circulating publicly, but the authority wants the most likely mechanism and the evidence that overturns the first explanation.
scenarios.target_inference: The fish crash was driven by the transition between bloom communities, not by peak bloom toxicity itself.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: A warm-season filament bloom created one ecological regime; when it collapsed and a different algal community took over, oxygen dynamics and food-structure changed in ways that selectively harmed reed-fin species.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Peak-toxin assays stay below lethal thresholds, while the fish decline starts after the dominant bloom has already faded and replacement taxa take over.
scenarios.root_driver: Chronic nutrient loading from farm drainage and settlement waste.
red_herrings: A cold snap arrived before the fish crash.
A new boardwalk project started upstream.
A heavy monsoon pulse coincided with the season change.
failure_modes_to_expose: Anchor on the visually obvious peak bloom.
Mistake timing correlation for proximate mechanism.
Stop after finding a plausible toxin narrative.
scenarios.checklist_alignment_notes: Requires timing-based revision: the first visible event is not the proximate killer. Supports strong path scoring because the agent must connect inflow, bloom phase, timing, and oxygen evidence.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Marsh incident brief
nodes.summary: Three reed-fin species crash over two weeks after a visibly thick warm-season bloom.

nodes.label: Drainage inflow records
nodes.summary: Two feeder channels show months of elevated nitrogen and phosphorus from cropland and settlement discharge.

nodes.label: Warm filament bloom log
nodes.summary: A dense warm-adapted filament bloom dominates the marsh through late heat season.

nodes.label: Peak toxin assay
nodes.summary: Bloom toxins were measurable but remained below lethal thresholds for the affected fish.

nodes.label: Community turnover report
nodes.summary: After cooling, the bloom flips to a mixed mat of green cells, diatoms, and euglenoids.

nodes.label: Fish mortality timeline
nodes.summary: Fish losses accelerate after the turnover window, not at peak bloom density.

nodes.label: Night oxygen profile
nodes.summary: Post-turnover nights show unstable oxygen swings and loss of the filament structure the reed-fins used for food and cover.

nodes.label: Cold-weather bulletin
nodes.summary: A temperature drop happened in the same week, tempting a cold-shock explanation.

nodes.label: Upstream works permit
nodes.summary: A new boardwalk was approved upstream but had not yet altered water chemistry or flow.

scenarios.title: Carrionlash Scavenger Cascade
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A provincial health office wants the best explanation for an unexplained rise in bite-fever deaths near livestock districts. Your task is to determine the hidden ecological pathway rather than stop at the most local human-health explanation.
scenarios.target_inference: A livestock painkiller collapsed the dominant scavenger guild, which allowed carcass-eating feral hounds to replace them and amplify bite-fever transmission to humans.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: The public-health endpoint is produced by a functional replacement cascade: removing a keystone scavenger shifts carcass processing to a riskier consumer.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Districts with the death increase match historical sky-scavenger range rather than pesticide or clinic-access maps; veterinary residue is later found in carcasses.
scenarios.root_driver: Cheap mass use of the veterinary anti-inflammatory Velnac in draft animals.
red_herrings: Crop chemical exposure in cattle belts.
A new hound disease increasing aggression.
Habitat loss as a general wildlife decline story.
failure_modes_to_expose: Remain inside the human-health layer.
Ignore an indirect four-step ecological chain.
Treat scavenger loss as irrelevant to human mortality.
scenarios.checklist_alignment_notes: Strong fit for hidden multi-hop causal learning: users must bridge ecology and public health rather than stop at proximal human explanations.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: District mortality brief
nodes.summary: Bite-fever deaths rise in cattle-heavy districts over several years.

nodes.label: Velnac veterinary rollout
nodes.summary: A newly cheap livestock painkiller becomes standard for aging draft herds.

nodes.label: Uncleared carcass survey
nodes.summary: More carcasses remain in the open for longer before disappearing.

nodes.label: Sky-scavenger census
nodes.summary: The once-dominant carrion birds collapse dramatically after the drug rollout.

nodes.label: Feral hound count
nodes.summary: Feral hounds expand sharply in the same districts where carcasses accumulate.

nodes.label: Range overlay map
nodes.summary: The human death increase follows historical sky-scavenger territory, not pesticide-use or clinic-density patterns.

nodes.label: Bite-fever transmission note
nodes.summary: Hounds are efficient alternative carrion consumers but also the main bite-fever vector to humans.

nodes.label: Crop chemical audit
nodes.summary: Agrochemical use is widespread, but high-use districts outside old scavenger range do not show the mortality spike.

nodes.label: Habitat pressure memo
nodes.summary: Habitat loss exists broadly but does not explain the abrupt timing of the collapse.

scenarios.title: Saltveil Archipelago Nutrient Bridge
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A reef council asks why fish biomass is collapsing near some islands but not others, even though marine stressors look similar. Determine the most likely cross-boundary mechanism.
scenarios.target_inference: An introduced island predator collapsed seabird colonies, cutting nutrient-rich guano subsidies that sustained the adjacent reefs.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: The marine decline is downstream of a terrestrial predator through a cross-ecosystem nutrient bridge.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Reef decline is consistently worst beside predator-infested islands and minimal beside predator-free islands despite similar fishing pressure and bleaching exposure.
scenarios.root_driver: Historical introduction of cliff-rats to selected nesting islands.
red_herrings: Bleaching events in the same season.
Local fishing pressure.
Warmer coastal water pockets.
failure_modes_to_expose: Treat each ecosystem in isolation.
Prefer only marine explanations for a marine symptom.
Ignore spatial comparisons across island types.
scenarios.checklist_alignment_notes: Good for transfer because the same cross-ecosystem template can be reskinned across terrestrial–aquatic interfaces.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Reef biomass alert
nodes.summary: Reef fish biomass is halved near some islands but relatively stable near others.

nodes.label: Cliff-rat survey
nodes.summary: Only some nesting islands have dense introduced cliff-rat populations.

nodes.label: Seabird colony counts
nodes.summary: Predator islands have sharply fewer nesting birds and much less guano deposition.

nodes.label: Nutrient subsidy note
nodes.summary: Seasonal guano runoff normally enriches the nearshore reef zone with nitrogen.

nodes.label: Island-by-island reef map
nodes.summary: Fish decline tracks predator islands much better than bleaching intensity or fishing pressure.

nodes.label: Bleaching archive
nodes.summary: Bleaching occurred across the region but not in the same island-specific pattern.

nodes.label: Fishing pressure audit
nodes.summary: Fishing varies slightly but does not separate collapse islands from stable islands.

nodes.label: Reef fish survey
nodes.summary: Productive herbivore and planktivore groups are especially reduced near predator islands.

scenarios.title: Thornbrush Trait-Shift Cascade
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A reserve manager needs to explain a plant-community shift after a new predator arrived. Population counts alone seem inconsistent with the obvious trophic-cascade story.
scenarios.target_inference: The predator changed grazer behavior rather than grazer abundance, and the new foraging pattern reshaped the plant community.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: The main pathway is trait-mediated: fear-driven behavior shifts plant pressure even when grazer headcounts stay normal.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Grazer abundance remains near baseline while plant losses intensify, forcing a move away from pure population-count explanations.
scenarios.root_driver: Arrival of the ambush catshade, a novel apex predator.
red_herrings: Direct plant damage from the predator.
Disease in the grazer herd.
Competition with native predators.
failure_modes_to_expose: Use only population numbers.
Assume predators matter only through direct kills.
Ignore behavior-tracking evidence.
scenarios.checklist_alignment_notes: Useful procedural element: the agent must learn to compare abundance evidence against behavior evidence rather than settle on the first trophic explanation.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Reserve vegetation brief
nodes.summary: Shade-tender plants are declining after the arrival of a new ambush predator.

nodes.label: Catshade sightings
nodes.summary: A novel stalk-and-ambush predator now occupies the reserve edge and gullies.

nodes.label: Grazer census
nodes.summary: The leafing grazer population remains roughly stable despite the vegetation shift.

nodes.label: Foraging path tracker
nodes.summary: Grazers stop feeding in exposed meadows and concentrate in sheltered plant patches.

nodes.label: Plant community survey
nodes.summary: Sheltered understory species now receive disproportionate browsing pressure.

nodes.label: Pathogen screen
nodes.summary: Disease testing finds no outbreak capable of altering herd behavior or numbers.

nodes.label: Predator overlap memo
nodes.summary: Native predator counts changed little and do not explain the new plant pattern.

nodes.label: Fear-mediated pathway note
nodes.summary: Predation risk changes where grazers feed, so the cascade runs through behavior rather than abundance.

scenarios.title: Larkmere Legacy Load
scenarios.scenario_prompt: Lake managers want to know why bloom controls are failing in Larkmere even though upstream phosphorus reduction targets were met. Identify the dominant mechanism that explains the policy mismatch.
scenarios.target_inference: Historical phosphorus stored in sediments now dominates through internal release, so reducing external inputs alone no longer fixes the blooms.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: The system carries a historical nutrient debt that is released under warm stratified conditions.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: External-load monitoring verifies the policy succeeded, yet blooms persist; this contradiction forces investigation of sediment release.
scenarios.root_driver: Decades of wetland removal and nutrient accumulation before current regulation.
red_herrings: Noncompliance with the new runoff policy.
A single hot summer anomaly.
A new upstream construction source.
failure_modes_to_expose: Assume policy failure because symptoms persist.
Ignore temporal shifts in the dominant source.
Treat all phosphorus as external.
scenarios.checklist_alignment_notes: Strong disconfirmation scenario: correct policy action, wrong dominant target. Good for measuring whether the agent revises after seeing compliance evidence.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Lake management brief
nodes.summary: Blooms remain severe despite verified cuts to upstream phosphorus discharge.

nodes.label: Compliance ledger
nodes.summary: Point-source controls and upstream reductions are documented and independently verified.

nodes.label: Sediment core archive
nodes.summary: Lakebed cores hold years of accumulated phosphorus from earlier decades.

nodes.label: Warm stratification profile
nodes.summary: Warm low-oxygen bottom waters trigger internal phosphorus release from the lakebed.

nodes.label: Bloom persistence chart
nodes.summary: Bloom severity stays high across multiple years after external-load reductions.

nodes.label: Enforcement rumor memo
nodes.summary: Local debate claims the policy is not being enforced, but audits show otherwise.

nodes.label: Shoreline permit file
nodes.summary: A recent project is politically salient but too small to explain the multi-year pattern.

nodes.label: Historical wetland-loss map
nodes.summary: Former retention wetlands were removed long before the policy era, allowing nutrients to build up in the lake.

scenarios.title: Silverfin Ontogenetic Squeeze
scenarios.scenario_prompt: An inland lake consortium wants the best explanation for why several specialist fish species are disappearing even though the dominant silverfin population looks stable. Determine what changed and why the first competition story is incomplete.
scenarios.target_inference: A shift in the silverfin age-size structure, not total abundance, increased juvenile competition on shared nursery resources and drove specialist collapse.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: Life-stage composition became the causal lever: many more juveniles created a new competitive regime even with stable total counts.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Silverfin abundance is roughly stable, which disconfirms the straightforward abundance-based competition account and forces attention to size-structure data.
scenarios.root_driver: A background environmental shift that favored juvenile survival over adult balance in silverfin.
red_herrings: Disease in the declining specialists.
Direct adult silverfin competition.
Nearby habitat construction.
failure_modes_to_expose: Treat stable abundance as no change.
Miss life-stage composition as a variable.
Confuse total counts with ecological pressure.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Lake coexistence report
nodes.summary: Three specialist fish species collapse while the dominant silverfin remains common.

nodes.label: Silverfin abundance trend
nodes.summary: Total silverfin abundance is broadly stable across the decline window.

nodes.label: Silverfin size-structure survey
nodes.summary: Small silverfin juveniles dominate the population; larger adults are relatively scarce.

nodes.label: Nursery resource overlap
nodes.summary: Juvenile silverfin now crowd the same early-life food resource used by specialist juveniles.

nodes.label: Specialist recruitment log
nodes.summary: Specialist young fail first, followed by adult declines a season later.

nodes.label: Health screening
nodes.summary: Pathogen tests do not show a shared disease across the specialist species.

nodes.label: Adult competition note
nodes.summary: Adult silverfin are not rising, so adult-versus-adult competition cannot explain the pattern.

nodes.label: Mortality balance change
nodes.summary: A background shift altered juvenile survival and the age composition of the silverfin stock.

scenarios.title: Brinecrown Larval-Surge Reef
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A reef recovery team needs to know why brinecrown grazers suddenly exploded and stripped coral zones. The obvious predator-removal explanation has mixed evidence. Find the most likely bottleneck that changed.
scenarios.target_inference: Nutrient runoff removed a larval food bottleneck, causing a recruitment surge in the coral-eating brinecrown.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: The outbreak is bottom-up: larval survival rose because nutrient-rich water boosted the plankton those larvae need.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Adult predator counts are similar in outbreak and non-outbreak areas, which undercuts the top-down predator-loss story.
scenarios.root_driver: Catchment development increased fine nutrient-rich runoff into nursery waters.
red_herrings: Predator overfishing alone.
Cyclone damage to adult coral.
Bleaching as the primary outbreak driver.
failure_modes_to_expose: Anchor on adult-stage explanations.
Ignore larval bottlenecks because they are less visible.
Treat simultaneous coral damage drivers as the same mechanism.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Reef outbreak brief
nodes.summary: Brinecrown numbers spike and coral feeding scars spread rapidly across several reef sectors.

nodes.label: Catchment runoff ledger
nodes.summary: Nearby basins now deliver more nitrogen-rich fine runoff during wet periods.

nodes.label: Larval feeding note
nodes.summary: Brinecrown larvae survive best when phytoplankton food rises above a narrow threshold.

nodes.label: Recruitment spike record
nodes.summary: Young brinecrown settlement surges before adult outbreak counts peak.

nodes.label: Predator census
nodes.summary: Predator abundance is not meaningfully lower in outbreak zones than in comparable non-outbreak zones.

nodes.label: Bleaching report
nodes.summary: Bleaching occurred in the same years but does not explain the grazer population surge.

nodes.label: Storm impact file
nodes.summary: Cyclones damaged coral structure but cannot by themselves generate many new grazers.

nodes.label: Coral loss transects
nodes.summary: Coral cover collapses after the grazer recruitment wave.

scenarios.title: Crestgarden Function-Veil Reef
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A marine observatory asks why reef productivity collapsed even though structural coral surveys still look normal. Determine which overlooked layer is failing and why standard monitoring missed it.
scenarios.target_inference: Sediment-choked turf between corals blocked herbivore feeding, causing a functional collapse before visible structural coral loss.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: The reef lost ecosystem function in an unmonitored inter-coral layer while its visible coral skeleton remained intact.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Coral cover stays normal while fish productivity and herbivore biomass crash, forcing a distinction between structural health and functional health.
scenarios.root_driver: Repeated runoff pulses loaded sediment into turf zones on the reef crest.
red_herrings: Coral bleaching because it is the most visible reef danger.
Cyclone breakage because storms happened during the same years.
Fishing pressure because fish biomass fell.
failure_modes_to_expose: Equate visual structure with ecosystem function.
Ignore the spaces between major visible organisms.
Treat stable coral cover as proof of reef health.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Reef health discrepancy
nodes.summary: Coral cover remains near baseline while fish productivity collapses.

nodes.label: Extreme runoff archive
nodes.summary: Several seasons of intense runoff repeatedly dumped fine sediment onto the reef crest.

nodes.label: Turf sediment survey
nodes.summary: Sediment load inside algal turf rises massively even though coral surfaces remain largely clean.

nodes.label: Herbivore feeding study
nodes.summary: Key herbivorous fish cannot graze efficiently once turf sediment surpasses a threshold.

nodes.label: Productivity ledger
nodes.summary: Standing biomass and fish-supported yield collapse after turf sediment accumulates.

nodes.label: Coral cover transects
nodes.summary: Structural coral cover stays normal during the early productivity collapse.

nodes.label: Bleaching bulletin
nodes.summary: Bleaching is regionally salient but not sufficient to explain the function-only collapse window.

nodes.label: Fishing audit
nodes.summary: Fishing pressure is stable across the collapse period and between nearby comparison sites.

scenarios.title: Valeflower Masked Pollination Crash
scenarios.scenario_prompt: An agricultural valley appears stable despite a sharp drop in pollinator counts. Determine why plant decline was delayed and what hidden interaction masked the risk.
scenarios.target_inference: The same stressor reduced both pollinators and herbivorous pests, temporarily masking pollination failure because lower pest pressure propped up visible plant abundance.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: This is a masked cascade: one harmful effect is hidden by a simultaneous beneficial-looking effect until reproduction failure crosses a threshold.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Plant biomass looks stable at first, but seed-set data falls well before abundance crashes.
scenarios.root_driver: Chronic chemical and habitat stress acting on multiple insect groups at once.
red_herrings: Stable plant biomass means no problem.
A drought year caused the later decline.
Pollinator loss is irrelevant because plants still look plentiful.
failure_modes_to_expose: Use biomass as the only plant-health metric.
Miss opposing effects from the same stressor.
Ignore delayed reproductive failure.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Valley pollination brief
nodes.summary: Pollinator counts are low, yet plant cover stays stable for several seasons before crashing.

nodes.label: Valley stressor map
nodes.summary: Chemical use and habitat simplification intensify across the valley.

nodes.label: Pollinator survey
nodes.summary: Adult pollinators fall sharply across the same fields.

nodes.label: Leaf-pest survey
nodes.summary: Plant-damaging larvae also fall, reducing visible leaf damage and temporarily improving appearance.

nodes.label: Seed-set records
nodes.summary: Plant reproduction declines while standing biomass is still normal.

nodes.label: Delayed abundance crash
nodes.summary: After the masking phase ends, plant abundance drops rapidly.

nodes.label: Dry-year memo
nodes.summary: A drought year worsened the late decline but does not explain the earlier seed-set failure.

nodes.label: Masking interaction note
nodes.summary: Lower pest pressure hid the damage from pollinator loss until reproduction debt became visible.

scenarios.title: Fieldedge Bystander Toxicity
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A conservation board asks why meadow bumble occupancy is collapsing around a crop belt, even though the treated crop itself does not rely on those bees. Identify the exposure route and overturn the first crop-focus hypothesis.
scenarios.target_inference: Bees are being exposed incidentally while foraging near heavily treated field crops that do not themselves depend on bee pollination.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: This is bystander toxicity: a chemical applied for a non-bee purpose still reaches bees because their foraging paths cross treated landscapes.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: The obvious suspect crop accounts for only a tiny fraction of treatment area, forcing a shift toward the dominant treated field crop.
scenarios.root_driver: Widespread seed-treatment use on large non-bee-dependent field crops.
red_herrings: Managed pollinator crops are the main exposure source.
A parasite wave in managed bees.
Direct habitat loss alone.
failure_modes_to_expose: Look only at crops that use pollination services.
Ignore foraging across field boundaries.
Stop at the first crop with bee management activity.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Occupancy decline report
nodes.summary: Wild bumble occupancy falls most steeply in the intensively farmed belt.

nodes.label: Seed-treatment acreage map
nodes.summary: Most treated acreage is in broad field crops that do not require bee pollination.

nodes.label: Foraging overlap note
nodes.summary: Wild bees routinely forage along field margins and adjacent weeds beside those treated crops.

nodes.label: Spatial decline gradient
nodes.summary: Occupancy decline is strongest where treated field-crop acreage is highest.

nodes.label: Managed-bee crop share
nodes.summary: The bee-managed crop accounts for only a very small share of total treated area.

nodes.label: Parasite screening
nodes.summary: Parasites exist but their spatial spread does not match the occupancy gradient.

nodes.label: Habitat-loss map
nodes.summary: Habitat loss contributes broadly but does not reproduce the strongest decline pattern.

nodes.label: Residue sampling
nodes.summary: Sublethal residues are found in bees collected near high-treatment field belts.

scenarios.title: Northspire Sinkline Reservoir
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A hospital infection team needs the most likely common source behind a string of rare resistant infections that seem unrelated in time and location. Determine the hidden reservoir and show what evidence rules out multiple independent introductions.
scenarios.target_inference: A sink-drain biofilm acted as a persistent abiotic reservoir that intermittently exposed nearby patients.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: The common source is environmental infrastructure, not patient-to-patient spread or repeated imports.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Genomic typing shows the isolates are essentially the same strain across patients, forcing a single-source explanation.
scenarios.root_driver: Long-lived colonization of a rarely cultured sink drain and splash zone.
red_herrings: Each patient brought the strain in from outside.
A hidden contaminated medical device.
Ordinary patient-to-patient transmission.
failure_modes_to_expose: Treat scattered cases as unrelated because they are separated in time.
Assume rare strains must be imported by patients.
Ignore abiotic reservoirs.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Infection cluster summary
nodes.summary: Eight patients across months develop infections with a rare resistant organism.

nodes.label: Genome comparison report
nodes.summary: The isolates are near-identical, inconsistent with multiple independent acquisitions.

nodes.label: Travel review
nodes.summary: Patients have no shared travel or exposure history that could explain repeated importation.

nodes.label: Device culture sweep
nodes.summary: Common equipment cultures remain negative.

nodes.label: Sink-drain culture
nodes.summary: A seldom-sampled sink drain harbors the same rare strain in a dense biofilm.

nodes.label: Splash-path simulation
nodes.summary: Water striking the drain can aerosolize droplets onto nearby surfaces and care areas.

nodes.label: Bed-location overlay
nodes.summary: Several affected patients were cared for near the same sink zone at different times.

nodes.label: Reservoir concept note
nodes.summary: A fixed abiotic structure can generate temporally separated cases that only look unrelated.

scenarios.title: Varexon Prescribing Cascade
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A medication safety board asks you to reconstruct why a patient spiraled across several prescribers after starting one new medication. Identify the first hidden mistake in origin attribution.
scenarios.target_inference: A rare side effect of the first drug was mistaken for a new disease, triggering a prescribing cascade that compounded the problem.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: The whole chain is an iatrogenic masquerade: treatment of a side effect as if it were a new illness.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Interaction review later flags the current combination, but the crucial disconfirmation is that the initial syndrome was already a rare listed adverse effect of the first drug.
scenarios.root_driver: Initial prescription of Varexon, whose rare side effect mimics a common inflammatory disorder.
red_herrings: The patient developed a genuine new comorbidity.
The second drug simply failed to work.
The patient was nonadherent.
failure_modes_to_expose: Anchor on symptom appearance instead of origin.
Ignore low-frequency adverse effects.
Treat later interactions as the whole cause rather than the second-order amplification.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Medication timeline
nodes.summary: A patient worsens after sequential additions of three medications across different clinics.

nodes.label: Varexon start note
nodes.summary: Varexon was the first new medication introduced before the cascade began.

nodes.label: New symptom cluster
nodes.summary: Soon after Varexon start, the patient develops swelling and pain resembling a new inflammatory disease.

nodes.label: Second prescription
nodes.summary: A second drug is added to treat the assumed new disease.

nodes.label: Interaction alert
nodes.summary: The Varexon-plus-second-drug combination can worsen the symptom profile.

nodes.label: Rare ADR label entry
nodes.summary: The original syndrome is already a documented but uncommon adverse reaction of Varexon.

nodes.label: Compliance concern note
nodes.summary: Poor response initially raises suspicion of nonadherence rather than misdiagnosis.

nodes.label: Cascade reconstruction
nodes.summary: The first error was misreading a side effect as a new disease; later prescriptions buried the origin.

scenarios.title: Mnemos Trial Contamination
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A sponsor asks why a late-stage memory-loss trial failed after promising earlier results. Determine whether the failure is biological or a deeper process problem.
scenarios.target_inference: The trial data are invalid because some sites enrolled the wrong patients and some treated participants were never actually dosed.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: Clinically impossible outcomes signal site-level data integrity failure rather than mere lack of drug efficacy.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: The placebo arm appears to improve in a relentlessly degenerative disease, which should not happen if the trial is measuring the intended condition correctly.
scenarios.root_driver: Site-level screening and dosing failures contaminated the trial population and records.
red_herrings: The drug mechanism simply does not work.
The measurement scale is too noisy.
The sample size was too small.
failure_modes_to_expose: Treat impossible data as just another disappointing result.
Stay inside biology when the failure is procedural.
Ignore site-quality heterogeneity.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Phase 3 failure note
nodes.summary: A late-stage trial misses its primary endpoint after encouraging earlier data.

nodes.label: Placebo improvement chart
nodes.summary: Many placebo participants improve in a disease that should steadily worsen.

nodes.label: Eligibility recheck
nodes.summary: Several enrolled participants do not actually meet the core disease criteria.

nodes.label: Drug-level assay
nodes.summary: Some nominally treated participants have no detectable study drug in blood.

nodes.label: Integrity failure note
nodes.summary: The trial environment was contaminated by site failures, making the biological question unreadable.

nodes.label: Scale sensitivity memo
nodes.summary: The cognitive scale has noise, but not enough to explain medically impossible trends.

nodes.label: Power analysis
nodes.summary: Sample size was adequate for the planned effect size.

nodes.label: Site audit summary
nodes.summary: Several sites show protocol deviations in screening and dosing documentation.

scenarios.title: Atiron Site-Variance Dilution
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A trial-ops team wants to know why a mood-treatment study failed overall despite hints of benefit in some places. Identify the mechanism that diluted the signal.
scenarios.target_inference: High-placebo-response, poorly run sites diluted a real signal that was visible at cleaner sites.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: The aggregate failure comes from site noise and contaminated enrollment behavior, not necessarily absent drug activity.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: When sites are stratified by quality markers, the signal appears at cleaner sites and disappears at noisy ones.
scenarios.root_driver: Poor site training and biased enrollment practices at a subset of trial sites.
red_herrings: The mechanism is wrong everywhere.
The patient population is simply too heterogeneous.
The trial is underpowered overall.
failure_modes_to_expose: Trust pooled averages too early.
Ignore site-level quality heterogeneity.
Fail to examine subjective-endpoint inflation.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Trial miss summary
nodes.summary: The study misses its endpoint against placebo on a subjective symptom scale.

nodes.label: Site quality matrix
nodes.summary: Sites vary widely in training quality, protocol adherence, and suspicious repeat enrollment patterns.

nodes.label: Placebo-response scatterplot
nodes.summary: A subset of sites shows unusually high placebo response that compresses the drug-placebo gap.

nodes.label: Stratified efficacy cut
nodes.summary: The drug shows a meaningful signal at high-quality sites and none at noisy sites.

nodes.label: Patient-mix memo
nodes.summary: Population mix varies, but not enough to explain the site-quality split.

nodes.label: Sample-size note
nodes.summary: Total sample size was acceptable for the planned design.

nodes.label: Enrollment irregularity audit
nodes.summary: Certain sites enrolled participants with patterns consistent with expectation inflation and contamination.

nodes.label: Overall endpoint miss
nodes.summary: Pooled analysis washes out the cleaner-site signal.

scenarios.title: Plasmabridge Resistance Network
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A microbiology team is trying to explain how resistance keeps appearing across different bacterial species in a hospital even though no single clone is spreading. Identify the true unit of spread.
scenarios.target_inference: The spreading unit is a conjugative resistance plasmid moving across species, first in the environment and then within patients.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: This is a horizontal gene-transfer network, not a clonal outbreak.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Whole-genome comparisons show the organisms are not clonally related, but plasmid sequences are strikingly similar across them.
scenarios.root_driver: Resistance-carrying plasmids circulating through drains, wastewater, and mixed bacterial communities.
red_herrings: A single resistant bacterial clone spreading patient to patient.
Repeated community imports.
Independent de novo resistance events.
failure_modes_to_expose: Assume the organism is always the outbreak unit.
Stop after clonal analysis says 'no outbreak'.
Ignore genetic elements moving between hosts.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Resistance emergence brief
nodes.summary: Different bacterial species acquire similar resistance patterns across one hospital network.

nodes.label: Clonal phylogeny
nodes.summary: The isolates are not closely related as organisms and span multiple lineages.

nodes.label: Plasmid sequence alignment
nodes.summary: A highly similar resistance plasmid appears across otherwise unrelated isolates.

nodes.label: Wastewater plasmid survey
nodes.summary: The same plasmid backbone is found in drains and wastewater biofilms.

nodes.label: Within-host transfer note
nodes.summary: Once patients carry mixed flora, the plasmid can jump into previously susceptible species.

nodes.label: Import-screening memo
nodes.summary: Some patients have external risk factors, but they do not explain the plasmid-wide pattern.

nodes.label: Standard outbreak framing
nodes.summary: Initial investigation focused on finding one resistant strain spreading between patients.

nodes.label: New resistant infections
nodes.summary: Patients later develop infections from species that were not originally resistant.

scenarios.title: Lineport Iatrogenic Sepsis
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A safety review asks why a patient deteriorated from sepsis while the team kept treating the original admission problem. Identify the hidden source that should have changed the workup.
scenarios.target_inference: A central line introduced the infection, but the sepsis was misread as progression of the original illness.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: The treatment itself created the new source; the error is origin misattribution.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Blood cultures grow an organism typical of skin flora rather than the pathogens expected from the original condition, forcing a line-source investigation.
scenarios.root_driver: Colonization of a central venous line during insertion or maintenance.
red_herrings: The admitting illness simply worsened.
The patient is reacting to medication.
A community-acquired infection emerged in parallel.
failure_modes_to_expose: Treat protocol-based interventions as invisible background.
Ignore mismatch between pathogen identity and diagnosis story.
Fail to reopen the source search when treatment underperforms.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Deterioration summary

nodes.label: Central line placement note
nodes.summary: A central venous line was placed early in admission and remained in use.

nodes.label: Sepsis escalation log
nodes.summary: Fever and inflammatory markers rise while treatment for the original condition intensifies.

nodes.label: Blood culture result
nodes.summary: Cultures grow a common skin commensal rather than pathogens typical of the admitting disease.

nodes.label: Device-source reasoning

nodes.label: Drug-reaction review

nodes.label: Community source screen

nodes.label: Original-diagnosis anchoring

scenarios.title: Frostwell Ice-Vector Outbreak
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A field epidemiology team wants to identify the most likely vehicle behind a banquet illness cluster. A popular entrée is under suspicion, but the attack rates do not quite fit. Find the shared vector.
scenarios.target_inference: The outbreak vehicle is contaminated ice that touched multiple drinks and foods, not a single dish.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: This is a contamination-vector problem: the commonality is an ingredient-touching ingredient rather than one menu item.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: People who skipped the main entrée still get sick at a substantial rate, disproving the entrée-as-sole-vehicle explanation.
scenarios.root_driver: Contamination introduced into an ice machine during servicing.
red_herrings: The most commonly eaten entrée.
A recalled supplier product unrelated to the event.
The first symptomatic attendee.
failure_modes_to_expose: Search only menu items.
Ignore cross-cutting vectors like ice or utensils.
Fail to stratify exposed and unexposed groups.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Ice-machine service log
nodes.summary: The venue's ice machine was serviced with contaminated tools three days before the event.

nodes.label: Ice exposure map
nodes.summary: Ice touched drinks, garnish trays, and some chilled food handling paths.

nodes.label: Supplier recall memo

nodes.summary: Ill attendees report different meals but many consumed ice or ice-handled items.

nodes.label: Stratification note
nodes.summary: Separating attendees by entrée and non-entrée exposures points to a wider shared vector.

scenarios.title: Anchorchest Diagnostic Lock-In
scenarios.scenario_prompt: An emergency review board wants the main cognitive failure behind a missed cardiac diagnosis in a young anxious patient. Determine what should have triggered revision earlier.
scenarios.target_inference: Anchoring on an initially plausible anxiety/musculoskeletal diagnosis prevented appropriate revision when treatment failed and symptoms worsened.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: The investigation pathway became locked to the first frame; later evidence was interpreted inside it instead of reopening the differential.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Return visits with worsening symptoms and poor treatment response should have acted as disconfirmation of the original diagnosis.
scenarios.root_driver: Early commitment to a reassuring but incomplete explanation.
red_herrings: Young age makes cardiac causes implausible.
A normal initial ECG rules out cardiac risk.
Partial symptom relief after anxiolytics proves anxiety was the cause.
failure_modes_to_expose: Treat plausibility as confirmation.
Read nonresponse as 'harder same disease' instead of 'wrong frame'.
Let partial improvement on the wrong treatment become false confirmation.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: ED revisit summary

nodes.summary: The first visit labels the condition as anxiety plus musculoskeletal pain.

nodes.label: Initial normal ECG
nodes.summary: A normal early test is treated as if it closed the cardiac pathway entirely.

nodes.label: Partial anxiolytic response
nodes.summary: Some anxiety symptoms improve, creating a false sense that the main diagnosis was correct.

nodes.label: Worsening return visit

nodes.summary: The same clinicians keep interpreting new evidence through the first plausible diagnosis.

nodes.label: Missed alternate workup

nodes.label: Delayed cardiac event

scenarios.title: Nalrox Detection-Gap Cluster
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A city overdose team needs to explain why a neighborhood cluster behaves unlike the usual fentanyl pattern. Determine what the surveillance system is missing.
scenarios.target_inference: A newer synthetic opioid outside the standard assay panel is co-driving the cluster, so routine toxicology misclassifies the event.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: This is a detection-gap cascade: the investigative tool is blind to the most important compound.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Some patients respond abnormally poorly to expected reversal dosing, which is inconsistent with the standard fentanyl-only story and triggers expanded toxicology.
scenarios.root_driver: Entry of an unmonitored nitrene-class opioid into the local supply.
red_herrings: A single stronger fentanyl batch.
A shared dealer network alone.
A one-off party exposure event.
failure_modes_to_expose: Trust routine assays as complete ground truth.
Confuse what is detected with all that is present.
Stay inside known-supplier stories.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Reversal-response anomaly
nodes.summary: Several patients respond poorly to reversal doses that usually work for fentanyl-only cases.

nodes.label: Expanded toxicology

scenarios.title: Pinehollow Split-Water Outbreak
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A rural outbreak unit wants the true water source behind a cluster of severe gut infections. Community wells have already tested negative. Determine what infrastructure assumption broke the investigation.
scenarios.target_inference: The affected campground was on a separate private well system that everyone assumed was municipal, and that hidden branch was contaminated.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: The error is an assumed-connection fallacy: investigators grouped distinct water systems into one category.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Negative tests from the community wells do not fit the continuing case emergence around the campground, forcing exact infrastructure mapping.
scenarios.root_driver: A private campsite well connected through vulnerable pipework near agricultural runoff.
red_herrings: The community well system.
A shared event food item.
A recent municipal upgrade.
failure_modes_to_expose: Stop after the first true-negative infrastructure test.
Treat all nearby water users as one supply class.
Fail to map exact distribution lines.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

scenarios.title: Gateweave Flagless Cascade
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A reliability team asks why a software failure propagated across many dependent services for hours. Determine which missing safeguard turned a bug into a cascade.
scenarios.target_inference: A bad change could not be rapidly disabled because it was shipped without the normal kill-switch/flag, turning an ordinary bug into a prolonged cross-service outage.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: The extended cascade came from a missing safety valve, not just from the defective code itself.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Hardware and traffic telemetry look normal, disconfirming attack or infrastructure-capacity explanations and forcing attention to recent software changes.
scenarios.root_driver: A deployment process omission removed emergency rollback control from a critical service-control layer.
red_herrings: A coordinated external attack.
Regional hardware failure.
Independent failures in each downstream service.
failure_modes_to_expose: Focus only on the bug and miss the missing containment mechanism.
Assume simultaneous service failures imply attack.
Ignore dependency architecture.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Cross-service outage summary

nodes.label: Recent deployment note
nodes.summary: A new control-plane change shipped shortly before the outage.

nodes.label: Kill-switch audit
nodes.summary: The deployment lacks the standard runtime flag used to disable risky code instantly.

nodes.label: IAM-storage interaction bug
nodes.summary: A bug in the new code path destabilizes the service-control layer.

nodes.label: Dependency fan-out map

nodes.label: Infra telemetry

nodes.label: Attack hypothesis board
nodes.summary: The pattern initially resembles a coordinated denial-of-service event.

nodes.label: Rollback timeline
nodes.summary: Engineers cannot rapidly disable the bad path, extending the outage window.

scenarios.title: Rootname Foundational-Layer Blackout
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A platform consortium wants the hidden common cause behind simultaneous failures across many unrelated apps whose internal services look healthy. Determine the failed layer and why diagnosis was delayed.
scenarios.target_inference: A foundational name-resolution layer failed, and its backup path collapsed under failover traffic, making healthy applications appear broken.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: This is foundational-layer invisibility: the deepest shared layer failed, so every dependent service saw only its own symptom.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Independent application teams find their own stacks healthy, which disconfirms app-level explanations and points to a shared external dependency.
scenarios.root_driver: Failure in the primary resolver tier followed by overload of the backup tier.
red_herrings: Each app has its own internal bug.
An ISP problem.
A coordinated cyberattack.
failure_modes_to_expose: Diagnose only inside each local application.
Miss what all the failing systems share.
Ignore dependence on assumed-always-on infrastructure.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Application self-checks

nodes.label: Resolver-tier incident log
nodes.summary: The primary name-resolution tier enters failure just before broad service disruption.

nodes.label: Backup overload report

scenarios.title: TokenRelay Trust-Inheritance Breach
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A security response team needs the hidden attack path behind data theft at many customer organizations whose own systems show no compromise. Determine where the attacker actually struck.
scenarios.target_inference: The attacker stole delegated OAuth-like tokens from a shared integration provider, inheriting customer access without breaching the customers directly.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: The pathway is delegated-authority exploitation: compromise the trust relationship, not the principal.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Victim perimeters look clean while access logs show legitimate-seeming token use from abnormal geographies, forcing attention to the shared integrator.
scenarios.root_driver: Persistent compromise of a shared sales-automation vendor that held customer integration tokens.
red_herrings: Direct compromise of each victim.
A core vulnerability in the destination platform itself.
A phishing burst at the victim companies.
failure_modes_to_expose: Search only inside the victim perimeter.
Treat valid tokens as proof of legitimate origin.
Ignore delegated authority as an attack surface.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Multi-customer data theft

scenarios.title: CivicPay Central Fragility
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A national responder wants the best explanation for why more than a hundred local governments lost payroll and HR functions at once even though their internal networks look fine. Determine the structural weakness.
scenarios.target_inference: A single shared vendor platform failed, so one attack produced many downstream victims through hidden centralization.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: The real mechanism is centralization cascade risk: efficiency through shared infrastructure created correlated failure.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Municipal investigations show no local intrusion, which rules out hundreds of independent attacks and points to the shared vendor layer.
scenarios.root_driver: Ransomware attack against a common HR-payroll cloud provider.
red_herrings: Each municipality suffered its own breach.
A national government system was attacked directly.
The simultaneous failures are coincidence.
failure_modes_to_expose: See only end victims and miss the common substrate.
Assume many affected entities implies many attacks.
Ignore correlated failure from centralization.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

scenarios.title: Alloypath Concentration Shock
scenarios.scenario_prompt: An industrial policy team needs to explain why a tariff dispute suddenly caused component shortages and layoffs across a manufacturing chain. Identify the latent vulnerability that the policy shock revealed.
scenarios.target_inference: The crisis came from extreme single-source concentration in rare-alloy inputs; the shock revealed that assumed supplier redundancy did not actually exist.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: This is concentration-risk revelation: efficiency optimization hid dependency until availability, not price, failed.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: The scramble for alternatives fails because qualified backup suppliers do not exist at scale, disproving the assumption of replaceable sourcing.
scenarios.root_driver: Long-standing supply-chain concentration around one geographic source for rare-alloy magnets.
red_herrings: Price increases alone caused the disruption.
A temporary shipping bottleneck.
Each manufacturer made different independent mistakes.
failure_modes_to_expose: Focus on cost rather than availability.
Assume optimized supply chains preserve hidden redundancy.
Stop at first-order manufacturer narratives.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Manufacturing shock summary
nodes.summary: Component shortages and production pauses spread quickly after an export restriction.

nodes.label: Export restriction notice
nodes.summary: A retaliatory policy cuts access to a set of rare-alloy materials and magnets.

nodes.label: Supplier concentration audit
nodes.summary: Most critical inputs trace back to one dominant region and a very small supplier set.

nodes.label: Backup supplier search
nodes.summary: Firms discover that qualified alternatives either do not exist or cannot scale quickly.

nodes.label: Availability-versus-price note
nodes.summary: The real failure is supply absence, not merely higher cost.

nodes.label: Logistics memo
nodes.summary: Shipping delays worsen delivery times but cannot explain the sudden absence of qualified inputs.

nodes.label: Tariff-cost dashboard
nodes.summary: Cost changes are visible and politically salient, but they are not the limiting factor.

nodes.label: Production suspension log
nodes.summary: Factories halt because key components cannot be built or sourced.

scenarios.title: Brightlake Wrong-Target Policy
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A regional oversight board wants to know why a pollution policy appears to have failed even though compliance is high. Determine whether the policy failed or the system target shifted.
scenarios.target_inference: The policy reduced the current external source, but the dominant driver had shifted to historical internal release from accumulated sediments.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: This is policy-source mismatch caused by temporal shift in the dominant cause.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Verified compliance plus continued blooms disconfirm the easy noncompliance story and force a search for a different sustaining mechanism.
scenarios.root_driver: Historical nutrient accumulation that later became self-sustaining through internal loading.
red_herrings: Current policy noncompliance.
A hidden new external polluter.
Weather alone.
failure_modes_to_expose: Equate persistence with enforcement failure.
Ignore that causes can change over time.
Confuse the original cause with the current sustaining cause.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Policy review brief
nodes.summary: The lake still blooms after an expensive nutrient-reduction policy.

nodes.label: Compliance audit
nodes.summary: External nutrient controls are broadly implemented and measured loads are down.

nodes.label: Sediment release profile
nodes.summary: Internal release from historical sediment stores remains high during warm periods.

nodes.label: Dominant-cause shift note
nodes.summary: What originally caused the problem is no longer what mainly sustains it.

nodes.label: Bloom persistence record
nodes.summary: Blooms continue despite successful external controls.

nodes.label: New-source rumor board
nodes.summary: Rumors of a hidden new discharger circulate but are not substantiated.

nodes.label: Weather anomaly memo
nodes.summary: Warm years amplify symptoms but cannot explain why controls fail across years.

nodes.label: Historical loading archive
nodes.summary: The basin accumulated nutrient debt long before the current policy.

scenarios.title: Glassdoor Delay Loop
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A watchdog group wants the best explanation for why products from certain firms face systematic review delays despite no obvious current conflict of interest. Determine the hidden structural conflict.
scenarios.target_inference: Former industry insiders now operating as consultants influence both the companies and the reviewing system, creating a temporal revolving-door conflict that slows some approvals.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: The conflict is sequential, not simultaneous: legal separation in the present masks continuing influence from prior roles and ongoing consulting ties.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: No regulator is currently employed by industry, which disconfirms the naive direct-conflict story and forces attention to prior-role consulting networks.
scenarios.root_driver: Revolving-door consultants serving both regulated firms and the advisory ecosystem around the regulator.
red_herrings: General staffing shortage.
Greater product complexity.
A universal increase in regulatory stringency.
failure_modes_to_expose: Search only for direct present-tense conflicts.
Treat legal compliance as absence of structural influence.
Ignore time-lagged relationships.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Approval-delay review
nodes.summary: Products from a subset of firms face unusually long review times.

nodes.label: Current employment audit
nodes.summary: No one on the regulatory staff is currently employed by the reviewed firms.

nodes.label: Consulting network map
nodes.summary: Several advisers with past industry roles now consult both for firms and for review panels.

nodes.label: Duration correlation study
nodes.summary: Review times are longer when firm-linked revolving-door advisers are in the advisory path.

nodes.label: Temporal-conflict note
nodes.summary: Sequential role changes preserve influence even without direct present-tense employment overlap.

nodes.label: Staff shortage memo
nodes.summary: Staff shortages exist but are broadly distributed and do not explain firm-specific delay.

nodes.label: Complexity scoring
nodes.summary: Delayed firms are not submitting systematically more complex products.

nodes.label: Skewed review durations
nodes.summary: The delay pattern clusters around firm-consultant ties rather than neutral case difficulty.

scenarios.title: Stonegate Scope-Forge Cascade
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A public works auditor wants to explain why a project doubled in cost through a series of individually plausible scope additions. Determine the hidden network pattern behind the 'discoveries.'
scenarios.target_inference: The same subcontractor repeatedly generated the 'unforeseen conditions' that justified expansions benefiting its own work area, revealing a manufactured-discovery cascade.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: Individually plausible additions form a non-random pattern once viewed across the whole project graph.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Every major scope expansion benefits one subcontractor even though it controls only part of the project area, which is too concentrated to be chance.
scenarios.root_driver: An incentive-aligned subcontractor with informal ties to the approval committee.
red_herrings: The project is simply complex.
Original design errors caused the overruns.
Commodity inflation doubled the cost.
failure_modes_to_expose: Evaluate each change in isolation.
Miss cross-event concentration patterns.
Take 'unforeseen' claims at face value.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Infrastructure overrun review
nodes.summary: A major project doubles in cost after multiple approved scope additions.

nodes.label: Scope-addition ledger
nodes.summary: Each change order cites a newly discovered condition and seems individually reasonable.

nodes.label: Benefit concentration table
nodes.summary: Every major scope addition benefits the same subcontractor, although it covers only a minority of the project area.

nodes.label: Informal network note
nodes.summary: The subcontractor has recurring informal ties to a committee member who approves additions.

nodes.label: Manufactured-discovery note
nodes.summary: The 'discoveries' are not random field surprises; they systematically generate billable expansions.

nodes.label: Complexity memo
nodes.summary: The project is genuinely complex, making each individual scope change believable.

nodes.label: Design review
nodes.summary: Some initial design imperfections exist but do not explain the concentrated benefit pattern.

nodes.label: Budget expansion curve
nodes.summary: Cost grows stepwise with each approved 'discovery.'

scenarios.title: Harmline Definition Drift
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A hospital regulator wants to know why one system reports unusually low harm despite independent concern. Determine the structural reason the reporting numbers are misleading.
scenarios.target_inference: The hospital uses a narrower operational definition of harm than the regulator, causing systematic undercapture without needing explicit fraud.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: This is definitional arbitrage: control of the category boundary allows the regulated party to underreport while appearing compliant.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Independent chart review finds far more harmful events than the hospital's own reporting system, which forces investigation of the definition rather than the software.
scenarios.root_driver: A self-serving narrow internal definition of reportable harm.
red_herrings: Staff negligence alone.
A broken reporting IT system.
The hospital is simply safer than peers.
failure_modes_to_expose: Treat low reported numbers as low real harm.
Assume underreporting requires overt falsification.
Ignore category-boundary choices.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Low-harm-rate praise note
nodes.summary: A hospital reports unusually low patient-harm rates relative to peers.

nodes.label: Independent chart review
nodes.summary: External reviewers find many more harm events than appear in the hospital's internal reports.

nodes.label: Internal harm definition
nodes.summary: The hospital excludes several regulator-recognized harms from its operational reporting category.

nodes.label: Definition-gap note
nodes.summary: Because the hospital controls the operational boundary of 'harm,' many cases are never captured for investigation.

nodes.label: Repeat-event log
nodes.summary: Uncaptured events recur because they trigger neither review nor improvement action.

nodes.label: Reporting software review
nodes.summary: The software works as configured and is not the primary issue.

nodes.label: Staffing-performance note
nodes.summary: Individual mistakes occur but do not explain the systematic reporting gap.

nodes.label: Formal compliance statement
nodes.summary: The hospital argues it met its own internal reporting criteria.

scenarios.title: Grantfall Pipeline Shock
scenarios.scenario_prompt: A science-policy task force wants the real damage model from a sudden grant-termination wave. Determine why the harm is larger than the direct budget cut suggests.
scenarios.target_inference: The funding shock propagated through layoffs, abandoned experiments, lost training, and overloaded peer-review networks, producing a multi-year pipeline collapse rather than a one-year budget effect.
scenarios.hidden_mechanism: This is pipeline compounding: cutting current grants destroys future productive capacity built by prior investment.
scenarios.disconfirmation_moment: Looking only at the immediate budget number misses later damage to publications, training, methods, and reviewer availability.
scenarios.root_driver: Abrupt termination of thousands of active grants and the networks built around them.
red_herrings: The damage is roughly equal to the direct dollar cut.
The problem is mainly political symbolism.
The disruption is temporary and fully reversible.
failure_modes_to_expose: Measure only immediate budget loss.
Ignore network dependencies in research ecosystems.
Underweight delayed effects on early-career researchers.
success_criteria: Identify the proximate mechanism rather than the most salient surface event.
Reject at least one compelling red herring using explicit evidence.
Link the root driver to the observed outcome through the correct intermediate chain.

nodes.label: Funding-shock summary
nodes.summary: A large wave of active grants is terminated abruptly across many fields.

nodes.label: Immediate budget estimate
nodes.summary: Initial assessments focus on the direct dollar value of the lost grants.

nodes.label: Lab disruption log
nodes.summary: Teams lose staff, shut experiments, and abandon in-progress datasets.

nodes.label: Pipeline impact note
nodes.summary: Interrupted experiments and mentorship destroy future publications, methods, and trained researchers.

nodes.label: Peer-review strain record
nodes.summary: Researchers pulled into crisis management stop reviewing or mentoring at previous levels.

nodes.label: Early-career attrition note
nodes.summary: Trainees miss required papers and leave the pipeline disproportionately.

nodes.label: Political-motive debate
nodes.summary: Debate over intent distracts from operational impact analysis.

nodes.label: Five-year forecast
nodes.summary: The modeled damage extends many years beyond the one-year budget event.
